exercises.title: Trace the INCR counter for rate limiting
exercises.scenario: Redis INCR atomically increments the counter — no race conditions.
exercises.expected_solution:
call 1: INCR returns 1 → count=1, limit=3 → ALLOWED. TTL set (count==1).
call 2: INCR returns 2 → count=2, limit=3 → ALLOWED. No TTL reset.
call 3: INCR returns 3 → count=3, limit=3 → ALLOWED (3 <= 3).
call 4: INCR returns 4 → count=4, limit=3 → REJECTED (4 > 3). Returns 429.

TTL set only on call 1 (count==1). Subsequent calls: TTL already set, don't reset.

Next minute:
  New key: 'rate:user:U001:28543211' (minute +1)
  Counter starts at 0 again
  First request: INCR → 1, TTL set
  User gets fresh 3 requests/minute quota

test_cases.expected:
call 1: INCR returns 1 → count=1, limit=3 → ALLOWED. TTL set (count==1).
call 2: INCR returns 2 → count=2, limit=3 → ALLOWED. No TTL reset.
call 3: INCR returns 3 → count=3, limit=3 → ALLOWED (3 <= 3).
call 4: INCR returns 4 → count=4, limit=3 → REJECTED (4 > 3). Returns 429.

TTL set only on call 1 (count==1). Subsequent calls: TTL already set, don't reset.

Next minute:
  New key: 'rate:user:U001:28543211' (minute +1)
  Counter starts at 0 again
  First request: INCR → 1, TTL set
  User gets fresh 3 requests/minute quota

hints: INCR returns the NEW value after incrementing
count==1 means first request in window; set TTL once only
New minute = new key suffix = fresh counter

exercises.title: Complete the rate limiter isAllowed method
exercises.scenario: Redis INCR-based fixed window rate limiter.

blanks.hint: Redis INCR method name in Spring
Sets TTL on a Redis key

hints: 60_000ms = 1 minute; dividing timestamp by this gives the minute number
increment() returns the new value after INCR
expire() sets how long the key lives before Redis deletes it automatically

exercises.title: Basic rate limiter with getRemainingRequests
exercises.scenario: RateLimiter service with isAllowed + remaining count.
exercises.instructions:
Write a BasicRateLimiter @Service:
1. isAllowed(userId, maxPerMinute): INCR + EXPIRE(count==1)
2. getRemainingRequests(userId, maxPerMinute): how many requests left
3. secondsUntilReset(): seconds until the current minute ends
4. getWindowKey(userId): returns the current key string
hints: currentWindow(): millis / 60_000 gives unique integer per minute
getRemainingRequests: read current count, subtract from max
secondsUntilReset: next window start - now, converted to seconds

exercises.title: Spring Filter returning HTTP 429
exercises.scenario: Rate limit enforcement at the Servlet filter level — earliest interception.
exercises.instructions:
Write RateLimitFilter implementing Jakarta Filter:
1. Inject BasicRateLimiter
2. Extract userId from 'X-User-Id' header (if missing: use IP)
3. Limit: 100 req/min for authenticated, 20 for anonymous
4. If exceeded: 429 with Retry-After header and JSON body
5. If allowed: set X-RateLimit-Remaining header and forward
hints: @Order(1): runs before other filters — rate limit check is the very first thing
return after writing 429: chain.doFilter MUST NOT be called after 429
Retry-After: standard HTTP header telling client when to retry

exercises.title: Per-endpoint rate limits
exercises.scenario: Different API endpoints need different rate limits.
exercises.instructions:
Write EndpointRateLimiter with different limits per path:
- '/api/orders' POST: 5/min (order creation)
- '/api/orders' GET: 60/min (order listing)
- '/api/products': 120/min (product reads)
- '/api/reports': 5/min (expensive operations)
- Default: 100/min

Key must include both userId AND endpoint to keep them independent.
hints: Key includes endpoint: separate Redis key = separate counter per endpoint
replaceAll for safe key: Redis keys can contain almost any char, but clean keys are easier to inspect
Method+path: POST /api/orders and GET /api/orders are different operations needing different limits

exercises.title: Zerodha order throttling — 3 orders/second
exercises.scenario: SEBI requires order rate limiting per second, not per minute.
exercises.instructions:
Write ZerodhaOrderThrottle:
1. isOrderAllowed(userId): 3 orders per SECOND limit
2. Key uses current SECOND bucket (not minute)
3. TTL: 2 seconds
4. Return remaining orders in current second
5. Explain the key difference from minute-based limiting
hints: millis / 1_000 = second since epoch (vs / 60_000 = minute since epoch)
2-second TTL for second-granularity limiting (covers edge case)
Same INCR pattern — just finer time window

exercises.title: Rate limit result record + header enrichment
exercises.scenario: Clean API with a result record enables easy header setting.
exercises.instructions:
Write a RateLimitService with a RateLimitResult record:
1. RateLimitResult(allowed, count, limit, remaining, resetInSeconds)
2. check(identifier, limitPerMinute): returns RateLimitResult
3. ResponseHeaderEnricher: adds X-RateLimit-* headers to responses
4. Show: how the filter uses RateLimitResult to set headers and write 429
5. Test: verify RateLimitResult.remaining decreases correctly
hints: record: immutable value object — perfect for rate limit results
remaining = max(0, limit - count): never negative
resetSeconds: next window start minus now in seconds

exercises.title: Sliding window rate limiter with Redis ZSet
exercises.scenario: Sliding window prevents the boundary spike problem of fixed windows.
exercises.instructions:
Write SlidingWindowRateLimiter:
1. isAllowed(identifier, maxRequests, windowSeconds): ZSet-based sliding window
2. ZADD timestamp as score
3. ZREMRANGEBYSCORE removes old entries
4. ZCARD counts entries in window
5. Test: verify no boundary spike (200 in 60s across fixed window boundary is caught)
6. Compare memory usage vs fixed window
hints: removeRangeByScore(key, 0, windowStart): removes all entries older than the window
zCard: counts remaining members (= requests in current window)
Score = timestamp: enables range-based cleanup; unique member avoids collisions

exercises.title: Rate limiter @SpringBootTest integration test
exercises.scenario: Verify rate limiter behavior end-to-end with MockMvc.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest integration test:
1. Make 5 requests to GET /api/products (limit=5/min)
2. Requests 1-5: assert HTTP 200 + X-RateLimit-Remaining header
3. Request 6: assert HTTP 429 + Retry-After header
4. Verify: Retry-After is a positive number of seconds
5. Use MockMvc for HTTP testing
hints: Use unique userId per test run to avoid interference between parallel tests
@BeforeEach cleanup: delete rate limit key before each test for clean state
header().string(name, matcher): Hamcrest matcher for header value verification

exercises.title: What is wrong with this rate limiter?
exercises.scenario: Common mistake: resetting TTL on every request.
exercises.expected_solution:
1. BUG: redis.expire() is called on EVERY request, not just the first.
   The TTL is reset on every single increment.

2. Does it reject requests? ONLY if the counter exceeds the limit WITHIN
   the 2-minute window... but:
   If the user makes ≤ limit requests during any 2-minute period:
   → The counter never exceeds limit
   → AND the TTL keeps being refreshed to 2 minutes from now
   → The key NEVER expires while traffic is active
   → The old window count stays in Redis indefinitely
   
   More precisely: the rate limiter DOES reject requests when count > limit,
   but the count never resets as long as the user keeps making requests.
   A user making 100 requests/minute on a 100/min limit would hit 429
   briefly, then... never reset (key won't expire while they keep calling).

3. Fix: only set TTL when count == 1:
   if (count == 1) {
       redis.expire(key, Duration.ofMinutes(2));
   }

4. Correct condition: count == 1 means this is the FIRST request in the window.
   The TTL is set once when the key is created.
   Subsequent requests increment the counter but don't touch the TTL.
   When the TTL expires (start of next window), the key is gone.
   The NEXT request creates a new key with a fresh counter.

test_cases.expected:
1. BUG: redis.expire() is called on EVERY request, not just the first.
   The TTL is reset on every single increment.

2. Does it reject requests? ONLY if the counter exceeds the limit WITHIN
   the 2-minute window... but:
   If the user makes ≤ limit requests during any 2-minute period:
   → The counter never exceeds limit
   → AND the TTL keeps being refreshed to 2 minutes from now
   → The key NEVER expires while traffic is active
   → The old window count stays in Redis indefinitely
   
   More precisely: the rate limiter DOES reject requests when count > limit,
   but the count never resets as long as the user keeps making requests.
   A user making 100 requests/minute on a 100/min limit would hit 429
   briefly, then... never reset (key won't expire while they keep calling).

3. Fix: only set TTL when count == 1:
   if (count == 1) {
       redis.expire(key, Duration.ofMinutes(2));
   }

4. Correct condition: count == 1 means this is the FIRST request in the window.
   The TTL is set once when the key is created.
   Subsequent requests increment the counter but don't touch the TTL.
   When the TTL expires (start of next window), the key is gone.
   The NEXT request creates a new key with a fresh counter.

hints: expire() resets the key's lifetime — call on every request = lifetime never starts
The counter does still reject when count > limit, but the window never resets
Fix: if (count == 1) sets TTL exactly once per window per user

exercises.title: Tiered rate limiting — free vs paid plans
exercises.scenario: Different user tiers get different rate limits.
exercises.instructions:
Write TieredRateLimitService:
1. UserTier enum: FREE(20/min), PRO(100/min), ENTERPRISE(1000/min)
2. PlanAwareRateLimiter.check(userId, tier): enforces tier's limit
3. Store tier in Redis Hash ('user:tier:{userId}', 'tier')
4. TierAwareFilter: reads tier from Redis and applies correct limit
5. Test: FREE user blocked at 21st request, PRO user allowed up to 100
hints: Tier-specific key: 'rate:FREE:U001:window' — if user upgrades, old FREE key still expires
UserTier.requestsPerMinute: enum with field makes limit lookup clean
Default FREE tier: handles new users not yet in the tier Hash

exercises.title: Global + per-user dual rate limiting
exercises.scenario: Protect against both individual abuse and overall system overload.
exercises.instructions:
Write DualRateLimiter:
1. Per-user: 100 requests/minute per userId
2. Global: 10,000 requests/minute across ALL users
3. Both use INCR pattern
4. Request rejected if EITHER limit exceeded
5. Return which limit was hit in the result
6. Test: show global limit is shared across all users
hints: Global key has no userId component: 'rate:global:{window}' — shared by all
Two increments per request: one per-user key + one global key
Return which limit hit: helps with debugging and correct error messaging

exercises.title: Rate limit whitelist and admin bypass
exercises.scenario: Internal services and admins need to bypass rate limits.
exercises.instructions:
Write RateLimitWithWhitelist:
1. Whitelist set stored in Redis Set ('rate:whitelist')
2. addToWhitelist(identifier): SADD
3. isWhitelisted(identifier): SISMEMBER
4. Filter: skip rate check if whitelisted
5. Admin endpoint to add/remove from whitelist
6. Test: whitelisted user bypasses limit, normal user still limited
hints: Redis Set: SADD adds, SISMEMBER checks membership, SREM removes
opsForSet().isMember(): returns Boolean (nullable) — use Boolean.TRUE.equals()
Whitelist check BEFORE rate limit: short-circuit for whitelisted callers

exercises.title: Rate limit dashboard endpoint
exercises.scenario: Show users their current rate limit status.
exercises.instructions:
Write a /api/rate-limit/status endpoint that returns:
1. Current request count in window
2. Limit
3. Remaining
4. Seconds until reset
5. Whether currently throttled

As JSON. Add a @RestController + RateLimitStatusService.
hints: GET: read-only — don't call increment() for status endpoint (don't consume a quota check)
resetInSeconds: next window start (window+1)*60000 minus current time in ms, divided by 1000
Map.of: clean way to build JSON response without a DTO class

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay API gateway rate limiter — synthesis
exercises.scenario: Production-grade rate limiting with tiered limits, endpoint-specific rules, whitelist, and 429 responses.
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Razorpay rate limiter:
1. MerchantTier enum: STARTUP(50/min), GROWTH(200/min), ENTERPRISE(2000/min)
2. Endpoint rules: /v1/payments/create=50%, /v1/refunds=20%, /v1/payments=100% of tier
3. RazorpayRateLimiter.check(merchantId, tier, endpoint): returns RateLimitResult
4. RateLimitFilter: extracts merchant ID from X-Razorpay-Merchant header
5. Internal whitelist: bypass rate limiting for internal service calls
6. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): merchant at limit gets 429, whitelisted bypasses

All compiles.
hints: Endpoint factor: /v1/payments/create gets 50% of tier limit (more restricted)
Whitelist check first: short-circuit before computing limit
Endpoint in key: separate counter per merchant+endpoint+window
